mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X1 20d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X1 20d engine typically costs £2,200–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. N47-era engines sit at the lower end (£2,200–£5,500), while B47 units run £2,800–£7,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. A used N47 family engine can significantly reduce costs compared to a brand-new unit. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X1 20d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which of the two engine generations your X1 20d has. The N47 (E84, 2009–2015) has a well-documented timing chain weakness — the chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making replacement an engine-out job. That said, the N47D20C 20d variant is regarded as the 'absolute top engine of this generation' and the 'longest-lasting engine of the first generation'. The B47 (F48, 2015+) is a significant improvement, but EGR cooler failure (subject to a major BMW recall) and DPF clogging remain common issues. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X1 20d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain failure on the N47 (pre-2015), EGR cooler failure on both N47 and B47 (subject to a 1.6 million vehicle recall), and DPF clogging (particularly with urban driving). The N47 timing chain is the most critical — it can snap without warning, causing catastrophic engine damage. Diesel variants have exhibited a higher incidence of faults compared to petrol versions.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X1 20d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept £6,000 X1 20d, a £4,000 recon is 67% of the car's value — borderline. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For B47-engined cars (2015+), higher vehicle values make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X1 20d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E84 (2009–2015) is N47D20C (177–184 bhp), F48 (2015–2022) is B47C20B (190 bhp). The X1 20d was fitted to both rear-wheel-drive E84 and front-wheel-drive F48 chassis. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N47 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£700–£4,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap requiring a full engine replacement. The timing chain shows the first signs of wear from 60,000–80,000 km. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Note that the N47's rear-mounted chain makes this an engine-out job.

eraMap.title: The 20d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Rear-mounted timing chain — the defining N47 weakness
Modular B47 — improved chain design, EGR/DPF remain issues

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B47 generation, the most common quote request today. Older N47 generations: see the Era Map above for era-specific pricing, which runs lower. A used N47 family engine for an E84 X1 can significantly reduce costs compared to a brand-new unit.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X1 20d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). N47 engines require additional labour due to the rear-mounted timing chain access — it's an engine-out job.

cta.label: → Compare X1 20d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Failure (E84 2009–2015)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power. The chain can snap without warning.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N47's timing chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making access impossible without removing the engine entirely. The chain's role is to synchronise the pistons and valves — if it snaps, the inlet and exhaust valves collide with the pistons, resulting in massive engine damage including blown pistons, bent valves, and shattered rocker arms. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Specialist quotes for chain replacement can range from £700 for independent specialists to £4,000 at main dealers.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Failure (N47 and B47)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, engine warning light, in severe cases, smouldering particles and fire risk.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — BMW recalled 1.6 million diesel vehicles globally over EGR cooler fire risk.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant loss can lead to overheating and engine damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over immediately.

problems.recommendation: Glycol leakage from the EGR cooler can occur, and in combination with soot deposits and high temperatures, this might result in smouldering particles. BMW's recall covers vehicles manufactured between May 2013 and July 2017. Check whether your X1 20d has had the recall work completed.

problems.title: DPF Clogging (N47 and B47)
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced performance, poor fuel economy, increased regeneration frequency, engine warning light.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — accelerated by short urban journeys.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (DPF clean or forced regeneration). A DPF clean can cost around £261.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring DPF issues can lead to turbocharger damage.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if DPF warning appears.

problems.recommendation: The X1 20d's DPF is sensitive to short journeys — the filter requires regular motorway runs to allow passive regeneration. If you primarily drive in town, the DPF will clog faster. A forced regeneration or DPF clean can restore function, but if ignored, the turbocharger can be damaged by excessive back pressure. Diesel models have exhibited a higher incidence of faults compared to petrol versions, with issues ranging from engine problems to DPF blockages.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Recall may cover it — check with BMW first
Forced regeneration or clean — do it when warning appears
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable if the rest of the vehicle is solid

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X1 20d? Check These First
buyIf: * 2015+ B47 with EGR recall completed — improved reliability over the N47
* N47 with documented timing chain replacement (especially over 60,000 miles)
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles
avoidIf: * Pre-2015 N47 with cold-start rattle and no chain history — the rear chain is an engine-out job
* No proof of EGR recall completion on any X1 20d
* DPF warning light or evidence of DPF issues
* Poor service history — both N47 and B47 are sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X1 20d is the core diesel variant across two generations of BMW's compact SUV. The E84 with the N47 (pre-2015) is the riskier buy — timing chain failure is common and expensive to fix, though the N47D20C variant is considered the "longest-lasting engine of the first generation" when properly maintained. The F48 with the B47 (2015+) is significantly improved, but EGR cooler and DPF issues remain. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,000 recon on a £6,000 X1 20d is 67% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully.

cta.label: → Compare X1 20d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X1 20d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (N47 timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (N47/B47) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Recall Check — Has the EGR cooler recall been completed? (1.6 million vehicles affected)
* DPF Status — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys accelerate clogging
* Smoke Check — White smoke could indicate EGR cooler coolant leak; black smoke could indicate DPF or injector issues
* Turbo Check — Test acceleration; any whistling, lag, or power loss?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
